Uphold (the crypto exchange) has a partnership checking account that is FDIC insured up to 2.25 million with deposits swept across 12 megabanks. It pays 3.75% APY paid out monthly which for you is almost $2k in annual interest just for holding with free ability to buy move to exchanges or brokerages to buy and sell and spend etc. with no lockups like a savings account that likely pays less. Most banks are paying literally .01% on deposits and are more exposed to systemic banking risk. Additionally, Uphold offers 2 or 4% (depending on what card you choose, the 2% is free but the 4% costs $100 per year) in XRP paid back on what you spend from your USD account, which is really nicely segmented from your interest account so you only want to keep in your other USD account what you intend to spend imminently and you can move between them in literally 10 seconds at a moment's notice. If you choose to use the account to deposit your paycheck you get another 4% in XRP, which is literally just like getting a 4% raise instantly and a 2-4% cut to expenses (other than credit card bills). So you get 3.75% on deposits held, 4% on deposited paycheck and 2-4% on what you spend. So far the most competitive way to hold cash I have seen. You could buy STRC and earn the divi payment as a cash position but if everything crashes crypto will also and you are massively risking your money by doing so. Hold strong cash and buy the real bottom.

Anything can be a store of value if we manage to organize a secure and efficient system of exchange. Yes gold is in principle as arbitrary as Bitcoin, but what people don't realize is just how difficult it is to come up with a practical medium of exchange. You need something that is scarce, secure, liquid and stable, because yeah obviously you just want something that just acts as a reference for value and nothing more.The "issue" with modern currencies that lolberts and schizos usually complain about is the idea of having centralized entities in charge of creating and regulating the medium, i.e. central banks. Bitcoin was supposedly an alternative under the delusional assumption that people were going to adopt it massively because muh banks are le evil. Now this is the crazy thing, suppose we do come together and say well lets adopt crypto universally as a system, the stuff with the transfer times and security is not even a big issue if enough people participate ( instead of what we spend on current currency infrastructure which still costs we spend it on crypto infrastructure in some way so enough people are mining all the time), the thing is why the fuck would people agree to use Bitcoin if already most of it is hoarded by a handful of people? We are by fiat allowing these hoarders to control the economy even though we are perfectly capable of creating a new crypto network? Just because it was first even if the code and idea is perfectly reproducibly? Except we are painfully retarded (which we are lol), there is no fucking reason to adopt Bitcoin. It's literally as retarded as someone buying gold at a stupid price hoarding it and expecting people to still buy it at that price because eventually we will adopt gold as THE medium of exchange.

Why do you retards reach for nonsense? The reason why gold and silver are money, is because they had value as a commodity before they became recognised as money. And no, their cake didn't come from some abstract financial mechanism that was just a Ponzi scheme like crypto is. If you bought all the gold, silver and copper in the world, the modern day tech simply could not be possible. The world was in the stone age or whatever before precious metals and technology. Prior to Bitcoin the world was working perfectly fine. If you had an the gold, silver and copper in the world, people would need that shit for modern technology, for electricity, for computers, if you have all the bitchcoin in the world, no one would even know or care, it doesn't affect anyone or anything because it's not even a real world commodity. It does nothing that can't be done better than gold, silver and copper, and it does nothing better than a crypto that was actually backed by Gold, silver and copper which places world limits on the currency itself because it's backed by something tangible.

Can't believe it still has to be spelled out for idiots, but if you read this and you still pretend that crypto is but a crypto scam where gamblers win at the expense of the majority, then yeah, you deserve crypto and I hope you put all your earnings into that.

>>61986899
This is peak delusion. BTC has never in the history of its existence touched the bottom of the previous halving bull run.
In order for the price to bottom out like that, we'd need similar but worse conditions. There was a bunch of crypto companies that went under during the crash of '22 because of over leveraged positions. They were the cause of the crash because they were all super overleveraged and lending to each other and created a debt bubble.

The only thing we'd have to worry about is MSTR defaulting on the loans used to buy BTC and having to dump the holdings. But he's not buying on margin, he's spot buying with convertible debt. And the maturity of his debt is between 2028-2032. So in all likelihood he's not going to be selling to settle his debt for at least 2 years.

The only real selling pressure that exists right now is weak hands and Bobos shorting. Bobo doesn't have the liquidity to dump the market to 20k. It's just not possible with the buy orders that are stretched down the order book.
And the weak hands are all small positions. They're not giga whales. And all the weak hands sold already.

Thank you for your attention on this matter.

item.title: WEAT - the play everyone is missing — 82 replies — 15.0/hr
item.content:encoded:
WEAT - the play everyone is missing

Open thread • Replies: 82 • Updated: 2026-03-15 15:13 UTC

OP

This is potentially the most consequential second-order effect from the oil crisis and almost nobody in retail trading is talking about it yet.
Oil and natural gas aren't just fuel — they're the foundational feedstock for nitrogen fertilizer.
The Haber-Bosch process converts natural gas into ammonia, which becomes urea, ammonium nitrate, and other fertilizers.
Roughly 2-3% of global energy consumption goes to fertilizer production alone. When natural gas prices doubled after Russia invaded Ukraine, fertilizer prices tripled and we got a global food price crisis.

Now consider: European natural gas has nearly doubled already in one week. Qatar - one of the world's largest LNG exporters - declared force majeure. 20% of global LNG transits Hormuz. Europe rebuilt its entire energy security strategy around LNG after cutting off Russian gas.

The third-order effect: We're entering spring planting season in the Northern Hemisphere
RIGHT NOW. Farmers make input decisions in March-April. If fertilizer prices spike 50-100% (which is beginning), farmers either plant less, use less fertilizer (lower yields), or absorb costs they can't recover. The food price inflation from this shows up in August-October harvests -
5-6 months from now.

They can release strategic oil reserves. There are no strategic fertilizer reserves. Food prices are about to be fucked, and as you can see from the chart - if you get in early, you can profit. Let retards from Reddit chase oil prices around. Go ahead and wait for them at the finish line.

You’re welcome fuckfaces

61986604
Yeah, the real play here is fertilizer. Most specifically urea based fertilizers. 50% of ALL global urea based fertilizer goes through the straight. Furthermore, it takes a good amount of oil to make it. The vast majority of this is made in asia, whose oil supply is totally fucked now. So, you need an oil company in a country that supplies its own oil, has plenty of open ports, and is not sanctioned to all hell and back. You're looking for an american company with maximum urea manufacturing capacity, which is CF Holdings. See pic related.

61986970
>>61957787
>The Haber-Bosch process converts natural gas into ammonia
No it doesn't. The Haber-Bosch process uses hydrogen and nitrogen. Natural gas can be used to produce the hydrogen, but the hydrogen can also be produced by other means.

Natural gas is not "the" foundational feedstock for nitrogen fertilizer. It is one option out of several. In China, for example, it is more common to produce hydrogen by using coal than by using natural gas. Of course, for plants that rely on natural gas, it is not possible to quickly convert to alternative sources of hydrogen, so there will be a supply shock.

>There are no strategic fertilizer reserves.
Not that it changes your central thesis, but this statement is not really true. For example, China has strategic sulfur reserves. Sulfuric acid is used in the production of phosphate fertilizers. However, those reserves are not large compared to global demand, and China has restricted exports.

61986973
>>61986604
>Most specifically urea based fertilizers.
All nitrogen fertilizers will be affected, not just urea. It is not just nitrogen fertilizers either. A large chunk of sulfur goes through the strait. Sulfur is used in the production of phosphate fertilizers

61986988
>>61958421
>You don’t mine nitrogen fertilizer.
Technically speaking, sodium nitrate for use in nitrogen fertilizer can be mined. However it is not a viable production method in this day and age.
